original_abstract: As the volume of manuscript submissions increases and new academic journals emerge, editors in political science have expressed growing concern over "reviewer fatigue"—the theory that scholars are becoming overwhelmed by and less willing to fulfill anonymous peer-review requests. This study provides an empirical investigation into these concerns by analyzing reviewer response data from the *American Political Science Review* (APSR) in 2013. We examine the rates at which scholars accept or decline invitations, the demographic and subfield variations in these responses, and the specific self-reported reasons provided for declining. Our findings indicate that while a significant portion of scholars cited heavy review loads, "reviewer fatigue" is only one factor among many. Results suggest that most scholars remain committed to professional service but must balance these requests against heavy workloads, administrative duties, and personal commitments. The paper concludes by suggesting that editors can mitigate recruitment challenges by diversifying their reviewer pools beyond "the usual suspects" and utilizing more personalized communication strategies.
humanized_abstract: Reviewer fatigue—a term used by political science journal editors to describe the growing belief that scholars have too many manuscripts to peer-review and therefore are less willing to accept requests for review. Does reviewer fatigue really exist? This article provides an empirical analysis of reviewer response data from one of the most prestigious political science journals, the American Political Science Review (APSR), for the year 2013. This data enables us to empirically document and analyze the extent to which scholars agree to or refuse to undertake requested reviews, the frequency with which certain types of scholars are solicited for reviews and their likelihood of agreeing to them, and the motivations of those who decline requested reviews. We find that a large majority of respondents who decline a requested review say they are too busy with reviews to undertake another review, but our results suggest that “reviewer fatigue” is only one of a host of possible factors that any given scholar might consider in deciding whether to agree to a journal request for a review. Based on an in-depth analysis of responses to an open-ended question in which scholars were invited to provide “any additional comments,” we find that most scholars consider themselves to be committed to the performance of the review function necessary to sustaining the journals upon which their research is published. At the same time, they all have extensive schedules to manage, schedules that are cluttered with reviews, research, grading papers, advising students, as well as family obligations. Our analysis suggests that journal editors can reduce their difficulties in attracting reviewers to the fold by thinking about the pool of potential reviewers in different terms than the traditional “list of people I turn to when I need a reviewer for this particular article.” It also suggests that they can facilitate more reviewer participation by streamlining the requests they send to scholars.
